title: Request: Using a Containerfile directly as an environment to run commands in
body:
### Feature request description

If you type `podman run -it .`, it errors, as `run` can only take an image reference. If you want to execute a Containerfile without building an image, your only option is to use `podman build --rm .`. This is not always a solution, however, because it doesn't allow either opening a shell or running different commands inside; the Containerfile is expected to build the project on its own, which would also be bad, because it makes it more complex to configure the build and it's difficult to run different tools.

I want the ability to use a Containerfile simply as an environment to run commands in, without needing to build an image, or needing to bake the commands into the Containerfile. Imagine this sequence:
```bash
podman build --rm -t temporary .
podman run --rm -it temporary
podman rmi temporary
```
This builds an image from ./Containerfile or ./Dockerfile, opens a shell in it, and deletes it. However, I've noticed that if you actually do this sequence, volumes mounted by the VOLUME command are not available to the shell opened by the `run` command. With the functionality I'm requesting, volumes should remain mounted when running the command, as if the command was run at the end of the Containerfile, rather than actually building an image and then using that as a base for a container.

This would allow containers to be used in a very straightforward way for development in CLI. A project developer can write a Containerfile that sets up the dev environment, rather than building the project entirely, and then they can simply:
- run `podman (run?) -it .` to build the container and enter its shell, where they can run `make` or whatever they want
- or run a tool, for example, `podman (run?) . make`
When these commands exit, the container should be deleted, so devs can do this across different projects without keeping track of anything.

### Suggest potential solution

Either `podman run` should also accept a reference to a local directory, or to avoid overloading it, a command should be introduced which acts similarly to `run` but uses a Containerfile as an environment rather than an image. An idea I had was `podman in`, as its purpose is running things `in` an environment. `podman in -it .` would load up the environment and run its default shell interactively, as `podman run -it <image>` does, and `podman in . <command>` would run one command in the environment.

### Have you considered any alternatives?

There are three possible workarounds: using dev containers, which can be integrated into an editor, but not all editors; a shell script, which is unfortunately not a cross-platform or cross-project solution; or building an image, which is completely unnecessary and explodes the length of the required command as you need to mention it by name. I think it's clear that this functionality would be useful beyond the workarounds.

### Additional context

My current use case for containers is to normalize dev environments, so that I can simply publish a project online with a Dockerfile/Containerfile and everyone can clone it and work inside it predictably. The step of making a Containerfile that sets up a dev environment is actually really easy! The friction appears when the user/developer, who is only required to have Docker or Podman installed, wants to use it. That is a bad thing, because the whole purpose of the container here is to make it as easy as possible for them.
```bash
~/code/project $ podman run . make
[...]
~/code/project $
```
It could be that simple!

comments.body: A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
